title: Vydia
subtitle: Music Technology Platform
description: Vydia began as a specialized video distribution platform. As the company expanded, it pivoted to become a comprehensive music technology platform — merging two distinct content types into a unified, intuitive experience.
problem: This platform change introduced significant complexity: merging two distinct content types (video and audio) into a unified, intuitive user experience while preserving seamless workflows for creators who handle both formats. Metadata management, release scheduling, rights handling, analytics, and delivery pipelines had to scale across vastly different partner requirements and user expectations.
process:
As Lead Product Designer, I kicked off the project by leading in-depth stakeholder interviews with our internal video and audio distribution teams. We mapped their key pain points with existing third-party audio platforms, uncovering recurring frustrations around metadata handling, workflow inefficiencies, and limited insights.

I then directed a thorough competitive audit of top players in the audio distribution space, dissecting interaction patterns, user flows, onboarding sequences, and data visualization approaches to identify best-in-class elements and opportunities for differentiation.

Building on these insights, we ran collaborative workshops asking a core question: "How can we make your daily work dramatically easier?" The result: a more intuitive, powerful audio distribution experience built specifically for our creators and our teams.
solution: The design process followed a four-phase approach, iterating rapidly between research insights and design execution. Each phase built upon validated learnings from the previous stage.
results: The redesigned Vydia dashboard launched with strong positive reception from internal teams, creators, and label partners. The overhaul transformed a fragmented experience into a seamless, unified platform for audio and video distribution—driving higher user satisfaction, faster workflows, and measurable business gains in operational efficiency, content quality, and partner ecosystem performance.
reflections: The project reinforced the importance of involving end-users early and continuously. The biggest wins came from simplifying complex workflows rather than adding new features.
leadership: Led a team of 3 designers through the full design process, from research to final handoff.

phases.title:
Discovery
& Audit
phases.desc: Conducted a comprehensive heuristic evaluation of the existing Vydia dashboard. Mapped 14 critical creator and label workflows, identifying 23 key usability friction points that slowed content delivery and increased errors.

phases.title:
Information
Architecture
phases.desc: Restructured the entire navigation model from a flat, cluttered hierarchy to a contextual, task-based architecture aligned with real creator workflows.

phases.title:
Visual
Design
phases.desc: Developed a refined design system with over 120 components tailored to Vydia's media-centric dashboard. Built a modular, atomic-inspired system enabling flexible customization and white-labeling for partner labels.

phases.title:
Testing
& Iteration
phases.desc: Conducted continual usability testing with internal teams, creators, and editors throughout the process. Achieved a 95% task-completion rate on the final prototype, with a 56% reduction in average upload time.

metrics.label: Decrease in the time to upload.

quote:
We are a progressive media company powered by the best-in-class content distribution and analytics software by way of our Vydia acquisition. This is why I fought so hard to buy a company like Vydia, because of the tech stack that they have that makes it almost like an enterprise software company.

- Larry Jackson, CEO gamma.

title: Fantasy Kooks
subtitle: Fantasy Surfing Platform
description: Fantasy Kooks is an engaging fantasy surfing platform that lets users compete in real-world WSL-inspired events. Users build teams within a budget to earn points from surfer heat scores, create custom single-event or full-season competitions, and play in bracket mode by predicting heat winners for escalating points and bragging rights.
problem: Traditional fantasy surfing games often feel rigid and limited to official tours, leaving fans wanting more control, creativity, and social competition—whether for a single heat, full season, or bracket-style predictions without complex salary caps.
process:
As Lead UI/UX Designer for Fantasy Kooks (live at kooks.vercel.app), I started with targeted research among surf fans, WSL viewers, and fantasy sports players. Interviews and surveys revealed frustrations with rigid official-tour formats, steep learning curves from complex salary caps, and limited social/custom options in existing platforms. These insights shaped the vision: a flexible, fun system where users could build budget-constrained teams for traditional scoring or predict brackets for quick wins—plus full control to create their own single-event or season-long competitions.

I then defined the information architecture and core flows, prioritizing intuitive drafting (with budget sliders and surfer previews), seamless event setup (drag-and-drop scheduling, invites, and rules), and an engaging bracket mode where correct heat predictions earn escalating points. Mobile-first considerations were central, ensuring live event tracking felt natural on phones during beach days or travel.

Rapid prototyping and iterative testing followed, with multiple rounds of usability sessions involving 15+ participants simulating real competitions. Feedback refined interactions—like clearer budget feedback, faster bracket navigation, and visual scoring cues—resulting in smoother onboarding, higher confidence in custom events, and a vibrant, wave-inspired design system that brings the energy of surfing to every leaderboard and heat update. Each step built on validated user input to deliver a distinctly community-driven experience.
solution: The platform delivers an intuitive, customizable experience with seamless budget-based drafting, drag-and-drop event setup for single heats or multi-event seasons, and an addictive bracket mode where accurate predictions multiply points and unlock leaderboards.
results: Users embraced the flexibility, with quick onboarding, high engagement during live events, and positive feedback on the social, creator-driven competitions that go beyond standard fantasy formats.
reflections: Simplifying complex fantasy mechanics while preserving excitement taught me the power of user empowerment in niche communities—custom events became the standout feature.
leadership: Sole designer leading end-to-end from concept to high-fidelity prototypes and user testing.

title: Hammer Surf School
subtitle: Custom Website & Booking Platform
description: A fully custom WordPress theme built for Hammer Surf School, the premier surf instruction experience on the New Jersey shore. The site showcases authentic lessons with Sam Hammer, streamlines class bookings across multiple beach locations, and connects seamlessly to third-party tools for a fast, mobile-first experience that drives real enrollments.
problem: The previous site felt outdated and slow, with fragmented booking across multiple beach locations, no unified scheduling, and poor mobile performance during peak summer season. Users struggled to quickly find lessons, view availability, or complete sign-ups, resulting in lost enrollments for kids, teens, adults, private coaching, and special programs like surf therapy.
process:
As Lead UI/UX Designer and Developer, I started with user interviews and competitor analysis of other East Coast surf schools to identify friction in class discovery, location selection, and booking. This led to a clean information architecture focused on quick navigation between group lessons, private sessions, and Costa Rica retreats.

I designed a vibrant, wave-inspired visual system with high-impact photography, then built the entire custom WordPress theme from scratch. Using Gulp for stylesheet and JavaScript compilation, I optimized every asset for lightning-fast delivery and seamless performance on mobile devices used at the beach.

Custom integrations were developed to connect directly with third-party booking and scheduling software, creating a unified "Book with Our New App" experience that pulls real-time availability across Spring Lake, Bay Head, Lavallette, Seaside Park, and private beach clubs. Rapid prototyping and testing with real students and parents refined the flow until booking felt effortless.
solution: The result is a modern, high-performance website that feels as energetic as the sport itself. Clean class grids, intuitive filters by age and location, real-time availability, and one-click enrollment via the integrated app make signing up simple while beautifully showcasing the authentic Hammer Surf School experience.
results: The new site launched to immediate positive feedback and measurable growth in summer enrollments. Faster load times, simplified booking, and a mobile-first design directly contributed to higher conversion rates and smoother operations for the school.
reflections: Building both the design and the code taught me the power of performance as part of the user experience. The biggest win was turning a fragmented booking process into a single, joyful flow that matches the fun of learning to surf.
leadership: Sole designer and developer responsible for end-to-end concept, custom WordPress theme development, Gulp pipeline setup, and third-party integrations.

quote: The new website captures exactly who we are — authentic, fun, and easy to join. Parents and students love how simple it is to book and see real-time availability.

title: Yashi
subtitle: Location-Based AdTech Platform — Acquired by Nexstar Broadcasting
description: Yashi was a location-targeted AdTech SaaS platform built for local broadcasters, agencies, and advertisers. As the first design hire, I built the design function from the ground up — owning platform UI/UX, a white-label system serving dozens of broadcast clients, and a signature live impression map that visualized campaign delivery in real time. In 2015, Yashi was acquired by Nexstar Broadcasting.
problem:
Local broadcasters and regional advertisers were being left behind by digital platforms built for national scale. Existing tools assumed technical sophistication — fragmented reporting, no real-time visibility into where ads were running, and campaign setup processes so complex they required constant account manager support just to operate.

Yashi's clients — NBC and CBS affiliates, regional agencies, and local businesses — needed something fundamentally different: a platform that felt powerful without feeling like enterprise software. It also had to serve radically different clients from a single codebase. Some needed full white-label branding to present as their own product; others accessed it through reseller portals. A UI that could flex across all of them — without feeling generic or requiring bespoke engineering for every client — was the foundational design challenge.
process:
I joined Yashi as Head of Design and the company's first dedicated design hire — responsible for building the design function, establishing visual direction, and shipping the platform alongside a lean engineering team.

I began with structured discovery across three distinct user groups: local broadcast sales teams who configured campaigns, media buyers who managed budgets and measured ROI, and internal account managers who spent hours onboarding new clients. Each group had fundamentally different mental models and workflow needs.

From these sessions, the core opportunity became clear: replace a fragmented, form-heavy setup experience with a modular campaign builder that surfaced complexity progressively. The live map concept came directly from advertiser interviews — the single most-requested missing capability was: 'Where are my ads actually running right now?'

I ran cross-functional workshops with engineering, sales, and product to translate AdTech constraints — geo-targeting APIs, real-time bidding data, impression tracking — into a visual language that made the platform feel approachable for non-technical clients. I also architected the white-label design system, creating a theming model that let account managers configure branded client environments in hours rather than days.

Throughout every sprint, I maintained tight partnership with the sales team — building pitch decks, demo environments, and marketing materials in parallel with product development. Design wasn't just shipped to engineers; it was actively used to close deals.
solution: A unified white-label platform built around three core experiences: a modular campaign builder with progressive geographic targeting and budget controls; a live interactive U.S. map showing impression delivery by location in real time; and a flexible design system enabling full client branding without engineering overhead. Every decision — from the map's dot-density visualization to the progressive disclosure of campaign settings — was grounded in what local broadcasters and advertisers actually needed to succeed.
results:
The live impression map became Yashi's most powerful sales asset — transforming abstract delivery data into something immediately visual and shareable in client meetings. Post-launch, daily platform usage more than tripled, campaign setup times dropped significantly, and client satisfaction with the map feature reached near-universal approval.

In 2015, Nexstar Broadcasting — one of the largest local broadcast groups in the U.S. — acquired Yashi. The platform's UX quality and technical differentiation were core to the deal. The white-label design system I built became the foundation for Nexstar's in-house advertising product.
reflections:
Building a design function from zero inside a growing startup taught me that design leadership isn't just about craft — it's about timing, prioritization, and knowing which battles to fight for. The live map almost didn't get built; I had to prototype it in Figma and make the business case to leadership before engineering time was committed. It became the product's signature feature and a key factor in the acquisition.

I also learned that at an early-stage company, design impact flows equally through the product and through enablement — the decks you build, the demos you configure, and the way you make a sales team feel confident showing the product to a room full of clients.
leadership: First design hire at Yashi — built the design function from the ground up. Owned all platform UI/UX, the white-label system design, live map visualization, and go-to-market design support across sales and marketing for the full duration of the company.
challenges: BUILDING THE DESIGN FUNCTION FROM ZERO
